title: Add and subtract mixed numbers with unlike denominators (regrouping)
description: Practice adding and subtracting mixed numbers with different denominators. Regrouping required.
topic_path: Module 3: Addition and subtractions of fractions
Topic C: Making like units numerically
Add and subtract mixed numbers with unlike denominators (regrouping)

hints:
Each mixed number has a $\blueE{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonE{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\maroonE{\dfrac{3}{8}} + \blueE7 \maroonE{\dfrac{5}{6}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonE{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueE{0} &\maroonE{\dfrac3{8}}\\\\
+&\blueE{7}&\maroonE{\dfrac5{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonE8$ and $\maroonE6$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonE{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueE{0} &\maroonE{\dfrac9{24}}\\\\
+&\blueE{7}&\maroonE{\dfrac{20}{24}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonE{\dfrac{29}{24}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonE{\dfrac{29}{24}} = \blueE{\dfrac{24}{24}} +  \maroonE{\dfrac{5}{24}} =\blueE1 \maroonE{\dfrac{5}{24}}$

We can replace $ \maroonE{\dfrac{29}{24}}$ with $\blueE1 \maroonE{\dfrac{5}{24}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueE1\\
&\blueE{0} &\maroonE{\dfrac9{24}}\\\\
+&\blueE{7}&\maroonE{\dfrac{20}{24}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonE{\dfrac{5}{24}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueE{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueE1\\
&\blueE{0} &\maroonE{\dfrac9{24}}\\\\
+&\blueE{7}&\maroonE{\dfrac{20}{24}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueE{8}&\maroonE{\dfrac{5}{24}}\\
\end{align}$
$\maroonE{\dfrac{3}{8}} + \blueE7 \maroonE{\dfrac{5}{6}} = \blueE{8} \maroonE{\dfrac{5}{24}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} + \blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{0} &\maroonD{\dfrac1{2}}\\\\
+&\blueD{4}&\maroonD{\dfrac5{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD2$ and $\maroonD8$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{0} &\maroonD{\dfrac4{8}}\\\\
+&\blueD{4}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{9}{8}} = \blueD{\dfrac{8}{8}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{9}{8}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{0} &\maroonD{\dfrac4{8}}\\\\
+&\blueD{4}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{0} &\maroonD{\dfrac4{8}}\\\\
+&\blueD{4}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD5&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\\
\end{align}$
$\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} + \blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}} = \blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac18}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{3} &\maroonD{\dfrac5{6}}\\\\
+&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac1{3}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD6$ and $\maroonD3$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{3} &\maroonD{\dfrac5{6}}\\\\
+&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{6}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{6}} = \blueD{\dfrac{6}{6}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{6}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{3} &\maroonD{\dfrac5{6}}\\\\
+&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{3} &\maroonD{\dfrac5{6}}\\\\
+&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD4&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}} = \blueD4\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD2\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}} + \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac2{3}}\\\\
+&\blueD{8}&\maroonD{\dfrac3{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD3$ and $\maroonD4$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac8{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{8}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{17}{12}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{17}{12}} = \blueD{\dfrac{12}{12}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{17}{12}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac8{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{8}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac8{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{8}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD{11}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD2\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}} + \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}} = \blueD{11}\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD4\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}} + \blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac3{5}}\\\\
+&\blueD{7}&\maroonD{\dfrac1{2}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD5$ and $\maroonD2$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac6{10}}\\\\
+&\blueD{7}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{11}{10}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{11}{10}} = \blueD{\dfrac{10}{10}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{11}{10}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac6{10}}\\\\
+&\blueD{7}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac6{10}}\\\\
+&\blueD{7}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD{12}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD4\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}} + \blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} =\blueD{12}\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}} + \blueD1\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac7{8}}\\\\
+&\blueD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac1{3}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD8$ and $\maroonD3$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{21}{24}}\\\\
+&\blueD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{24}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{29}{24}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{29}{24}} = \blueD{\dfrac{24}{24}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{24}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{24}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{29}{24}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{24}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{21}{24}}\\\\
+&\blueD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{24}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{24}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{21}{24}}\\\\
+&\blueD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{24}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD7&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{24}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}} + \blueD1\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}} = \blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{24}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD6\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} + \blueD9\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac1{4}}\\\\
+&\blueD{9}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD4$ and $\maroonD5$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{20}}\\\\
+&\blueD{9}&\maroonD{\dfrac{16}{20}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{21}{20}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{21}{20}} = \blueD{\dfrac{20}{20}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{21}{20}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{20}}\\\\
+&\blueD{9}&\maroonD{\dfrac{16}{20}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{20}}\\\\
+&\blueD{9}&\maroonD{\dfrac{16}{20}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD{16}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD6\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} + \blueD9\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}} = \blueD{16}\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{20}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}} + \blueD6\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\maroonD{\dfrac2{9}}\\\\
+&\blueD{6}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD9$ and $\maroonD6$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{18}}\\\\
+&\blueD{6}&\maroonD{\dfrac{15}{18}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{19}{18}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{19}{18}} = \blueD{\dfrac{18}{18}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{19}{18}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{7} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{18}}\\\\
+&\blueD{6}&\maroonD{\dfrac{15}{18}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{7} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{18}}\\\\
+&\blueD{6}&\maroonD{\dfrac{15}{18}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD{14}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}} + \blueD6\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}} = \blueD{14}\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{18}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} + \blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$

First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac1{4}}\\\\
+&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD4$ and $\maroonD6$?

Now, we can add our $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{10}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{13}{12}}\\
\end{align}$

Our numerator is larger than our denominator so our fraction is greater than 1. Let's rewrite our improper fraction as a mixed number. 

$ \maroonD{\dfrac{13}{12}} = \blueD{\dfrac{12}{12}} +  \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}} =\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$

We can replace $ \maroonD{\dfrac{13}{12}}$ with $\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{10}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD1\\
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{10}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD{12}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}\\
\end{align}$
$\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} + \blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}} = \blueD{12}\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{4} \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD3$ and $\maroonD4$?

Now, let's rewrite our fractions with a common denominator.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{12}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$


We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{12}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{12}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}$.

Now, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{3}}{\cancel{\blueD{4}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{20}{\cancel{8}}}{12}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{11}{12}}
\end{align}$

Next, let's subtract the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{3}}{\cancel{\blueD{4}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{20}{\cancel{8}}}{12}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&\blueD3&\maroonD{\dfrac{11}{12}}
\end{align}$
$\blueD{4} \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}} = \blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{11}{12}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{8} \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD{2}$ and $\maroonD5$?

Now, let's rewrite our fractions with a common denominator.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$


We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{10}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{10}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$.

Now, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{7}}{\cancel{\blueD{8}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{15}{\cancel{5}}}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}
\end{align}$

Next, let's subtract the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{7}}{\cancel{\blueD{8}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{15}{\cancel{5}}}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&\blueD7&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}
\end{align}$
$\blueD{8} \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}} = \blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{15} \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{15} &\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD{10}$ and $\maroonD5$?

Now, let's rewrite our fractions with a common denominator.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{15} &\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$


We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$.

Now, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{14}}{\cancel{\blueD{15}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{13}{\cancel{3}}}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}
\end{align}$

Next, let's subtract the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{14}}{\cancel{\blueD{15}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{13}{\cancel{3}}}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&\blueD{14}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}
\end{align}$
$\blueD{15} \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}} = \blueD{14}\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{17} \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{9}} - \blueD{12}\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{17} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{9}}\\\\
-&\blueD{12}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD9$ and $\maroonD3$?

Now, let's rewrite our fractions with a common denominator.  

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{17} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{9}}\\\\
-&\blueD{12}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{9}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$


We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{9}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{9}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{9}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{9}}$.

Now, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{16}}{\cancel{\blueD{17}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{13}{\cancel{4}}}{9}}\\\\
-&\blueD{12}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{9}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac79}
\end{align}$

Next, let's subtract the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD{16}}{\cancel{\blueD{17}}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{13}{\cancel{4}}}{9}}\\\\
-&\blueD{12}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{9}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&\,\,\,\,\blueD4&\maroonD{\dfrac79}
\end{align}$
$\blueD{17} \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{9}} - \blueD{12}\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{3}} = \blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{9}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{6} \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} - \blueD{3}\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD{4}$ and $\maroonD8$?

Now, let's rewrite our fractions with a common denominator.  

$\begin{align}

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$.

&&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}
\end{align}$

\hline{}\\\\
&\blueD2&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}
\end{align}$
$\blueD{6} \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} - \blueD{3}\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}} = \blueD2\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$
